source.title: An endocannabinoid mechanism for stress-induced analgesia.

confidence_rationale: This paper provides the behavioral and pharmacological evidence for the PAG as a critical locus for endogenous analgesia during stress/goal-conflict states, and establishes that this mechanism is opioid-independent. The opioid independence is significant for SD-021 because it demonstrates the generality of PAG-mediated descending inhibition beyond the opioid system. Endocannabinoid release in the PAG in response to stress maps onto the idea that aversive/high-stakes contexts (approximating commitment to harm-accepting trajectories) trigger the very molecular machinery of the descending inhibitory pathway. Transfer risk is moderate: stress-induced analgesia is not identical to commitment-gated analgesia -- stress can occur without planning horizon commitment, and the endocannabinoid mechanism may not be the primary mediator in voluntary goal-directed contexts. However, the PAG localization and the triggering by aversive context are directly relevant to SD-021.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Non-opioid stress-induced analgesia is mediated by PAG release of 2-AG and anandamide acting on CB1 receptors; CB1 blockade in PAG prevents SIA.
mapping.ree_translation: In SD-021 terms, the PAG is the relay node where top-down signals from pgACC (E3 commitment output) engage the descending inhibitory pathway. This paper shows that the PAG is also capable of locally generating analgesia-mediating molecules (endocannabinoids) in response to aversive context -- without requiring cortical opioid release. This supports the principle that commitment-gated analgesia can engage multiple molecular mechanisms at the PAG relay.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Stress-induced analgesia is triggered by aversive context, not by intentional trajectory commitment. Rodent model; endocannabinoid-mediated SIA may play a different role than opioidergic analgesia in humans. The paper does not address the selectivity of sensory-discriminative vs affective-motivational suppression.
mapping.source_context: Rat model; continuous foot shock as stressor; CB1 receptor antagonism (SR141716A) injected directly into PAG; 2-AG and anandamide measured by mass spectrometry in midbrain PAG extracts.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality Nature paper with causal pharmacological evidence; mapping fidelity is moderate because SIA mechanism (stress + endocannabinoid) differs from commitment-gated precision modulation; the PAG localization is directly supportive but the opioid-independence complicates translation to human voluntary analgesia
